prompt: Describe a dinner party you would host for five interesting guests (fictional, historical, or alive). Who would they be and what would you serve?
response:
Dinner is at eight, on the roof of my building in Brooklyn.  
The table is a single plank of reclaimed chestnut lit by a run of naked bulbs; beyond the parapet the Manhattan skyline flickers like a votive candle. Five place cards wait, each name handwritten in iron-gall ink that will disappear before dessert—ink that remembers it was once a living thing.

1. Leonardo da Vinci – invited because curiosity itself should have a body, and his never quite stayed in one century.  
2. Octavia E. Butler – to keep the future honest and the past uneasy.  
3. Sherlock Holmes (the one who never lived, and therefore never died) – because observation is a form of courtesy.  
4. Sally Ride – the first American woman in orbit, who saw the whole world shrink to a palm-sized swirl of indigo and cloud.  
5. King池中莲 (Yuèxià Lián), a 12th-century Song-dynasty courtesan-poet whose collected works were lost when the imperial library burned—she arrives through the cigarette burn in history where her book used to be.

I seat Leonardo at the north end so the wind off the river can flirt with his beard; Holmes claims the east chair, back to the city, claiming it improves the sightlines. Butler and Ride discover within minutes that they both kept notebooks labeled “What If?” and trade them like children swapping marbles. Yuèxià Lián says almost nothing, but every time a glass is refilled she tilts it so the liquid crescent matches the moon overhead, and the conversation hushes itself to watch.

Menu (printed on paper that dissolves in hot water, so the guests can rinse their fingers and the evidence at once):

First course  
“Still-Life That Moves” – a single ripe fig injected tableside with warm gorgonzola vapor; when the fork pierces it, the fig sighs open like a textbook heart. Served with a thimble of chilled whey sprinkled with meteorite dust—Leonardo insists on tasting the dust first, declares it “old star, perhaps cousin to the one that weighed on Machiavelli’s brow.”

Second course  
“Subduction Plate” – a slab of cedar plank that has spent six hours smoking above a lava stone. The plank is the plate; the salmon that once lay on it has been dissolved into an aspic that resembles the Atlantic at 30,000 feet. You eat the plank with a carbon-steel scraper shaved from an old subway rail. Holmes identifies the rail line by the metallic after-note: “IND, post-’42, southern branch.” He looks pleased for 0.7 seconds.

Intermezzo  
A sphere of clarified tomato water that looks empty until your tongue warms it; then it blooms into the memory of every summer you almost tasted. The sphere is served on the back of your own hand—you
